terms.definition: In this lesson, collecting and interpreting evidence about current knowledge or task performance, including the conditions, response, score, and confidence judgment.
In this lesson, selecting or changing a learning action in response to a monitoring judgment and then collecting new evidence from the result.

In this lesson, a task-bounded prediction made after committing a response but before feedback of the percentage of rubric points the response will earn; for an all-or-none 0/100 rubric, a probability of full credit can be expressed as predicted points on that same scale, but one realized item cannot show the quality of that probability judgment.

In this lesson, the relation between predicted and observed performance under the same declared 0-100 scoring rule. For one analytic-rubric task, the signed gap describes only that task. Under the all-or-none probability-to-points convention, a single gap records the realized outcome rather than judgment quality; only an average signed gap across several comparable all-or-none items can indicate aggregate calibration direction. Research may use other repeated-judgment measures.

Predicted percentage of points minus observed percentage of points under one matched scoring rule; a positive value means the prediction exceeded the realized result and a negative value means it was lower. For one all-or-none item, that direction reflects the binary outcome rather than judgment quality; calibration direction is interpreted only from the average gap across several comparable all-or-none items.
The earliest operation in the lesson's interpret–retrieve–select–execute–verify chain whose required evidence is absent or contradicted in the current task record.
A provisional action-oriented label for the first unsupported operation in a bounded task record, not an exhaustive cause or a label for the learner.
A structured record linking task conditions and attempt evidence to a provisional error hypothesis, confidence calibration, a targeted learning action, and a fresh probe.
A study activity selected because it directly exercises the operation currently proposed as the first broken link.
A new attempt, usually varied or delayed, designed to reveal whether a targeted repair changed the relevant operation and whether the earlier diagnosis should be retained or revised.
